Number: Four is the number of structure: four corners, four elements, four seasons, four directions. The tetrad is the square, stability made visible. Four emblems arranged symmetrically signal consolidation, foundation, the pause after initial movement.

Four batons forming a stable frame: the celebration of completed foundation, the gate or threshold of a new domain claimed.

Number: Five disrupts the stability of four: the pentad adds a fifth term that cannot fit the square. In Neoplatonic tradition five is the number of life (quintessence, the fifth element). In TdM pips five is often asymmetrical — one emblem off-centre — signalling instability, challenge, or the vitality that comes from disequilibrium.

Five batons in active disorder: competition as energy, the scramble before order reasserts. Creative conflict among equals.
sections.Scene: The Five of Batons (Cinq de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows five knotted club pips in an asymmetric arrangement — the mid-point disruption of the creative suit.

Number: Six is harmony restored after the disruption of five: the hexad is the Star of David, two triangles interlocked, the perfect balance of ascending and descending forces. Six emblems in TdM tend toward even, bilateral symmetry — equilibrium, beauty, reciprocity.

Six batons in triumphal arrangement: the victory procession, the will that has succeeded and is acknowledged. Progress crowned.
sections.Scene: The Six of Batons (Six de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows six knotted club pips in even rows — the balanced creative abundance of the suit at six.

Number: Seven is the mystical number: seven planets, seven days, seven liberal arts. The heptad cannot be divided evenly into the decad — it stands alone, indivisible, sacred. Seven emblems in TdM often create an asymmetric or pyramidal arrangement, suggesting abundance tipping toward excess, or achievement that has not yet found its resting form.

Seven batons with one baton raised against six: the defender holding ground, will tested by accumulated opposition. Courage under pressure.
sections.Scene: The Seven of Batons (Sept de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows seven knotted club pips in an asymmetric arrangement — the creative suit at its threshold, courage and persistence before the even eight.

Number: Eight is the number of regeneration and power: 2³, the first cubic number, the octave that returns to the root. In Pythagorean number mysticism eight belongs to justice and balance. Eight emblems fill the card almost completely — the suit energy is near its full expression before the density of nine.

Eight batons in parallel flight: rapid movement, messages in motion, energy that has found its vector and moves without obstacle.
sections.Scene: The Eight of Batons (Huit de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows eight knotted club pips in even pairs or rows — the swift doubling of the creative suit, velocity of motion and communication.

Nine batons with one upright and eight in row: the will that has endured, scarred but not broken. Perseverance at its limit — one more threshold.

sections.Waite:
WANDS

Page

In a scene similar to the former, a young man stands in the act of proclamation. He is unknown but
faithful, and his tidings are strange. Divinatory Meanings : Dark young man, faithful, a lover, an
envoy, a postman. Beside a man, he will bear favourable testimony concerning him. A dangerous
rival, if followed by the Page of Cups. Has the chief qualities of his suit. He may signify family
intelligence. Reversed : Anecdotes, announcements, evil news. Also indecision and the instability
which accompanies it.

sections.Waite:
WANDS

Knight

He is shewn as if upon a journey, armed with a short wand, and although mailed is not on a
warlike errand. He is passing mounds or pyramids. The motion of the horse is a key to the
character of its rider, and suggests the precipitate mood, or things connected therewith. Divinatory
Meanings : Departure, absence, flight, emigration. A dark young man, friendly. Change of
residence. Reversed : Rupture, division, interruption, discord,

sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Symbol:
Suit: Fire. The baton (bâton) is a living staff — Conver's version shows knotted wood, branches not yet cut from the tree, suggesting organic growth and raw vitality. Fire element: the impulse that initiates, the spark before the plan. The batons interweave like vines, dynamic and entangled.

Number: The Queen holds the suit from within: she is the receptive, inward expression of its power — not passive but internalising, cultivating, sustaining. In TdM the Queen faces left (inward) while the King often faces right (outward). She governs through depth rather than declaration.
sections.Scene: The Reine de Bâtons (Queen of Batons) in the Conver 1760 shows a crowned female figure enthroned, holding a knotted club upright and facing forward with confident warmth. Her court dress in the Marseille primary colors reflects the high feminine authority of the creative suit — enthusiasm, generosity, and warm creative power.
sections.Waite:
WANDS

Queen

The Wands throughout this suit are always in leaf, as it is a suit of life and animation. Emotionally
and otherwise, the Queen's personality corresponds to that of the King, but is more magnetic.
Divinatory Meanings : A dark woman, countrywoman, friendly, chaste, loving, honourable. If the
card beside her signifies a man, she is well disposed towards him; if a woman, she is interested in
the Querent. Also, love of money, or a certain success in business. Reversed : Good, economical,
obliging, serviceable. Signifies also— but in certain positions and in the neighbourhood of other
cards tending in such directions— opposition, jealousy, even deceit and infidelity.

sections.Iconography: King of Batons (Roi): a standing or seated court figure of the Marseille pattern bearing the baton emblem, in the flat bold colours of the woodcut.
sections.Upright: King of Batons — the outward authority of the suit — it commanded and ruled, within will, work, enterprise, and growth.
sections.Reversed: The same energy obstructed, immature, or turned against itself.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Symbol:
Suit: Fire. The baton (bâton) is a living staff — Conver's version shows knotted wood, branches not yet cut from the tree, suggesting organic growth and raw vitality. Fire element: the impulse that initiates, the spark before the plan. The batons interweave like vines, dynamic and entangled.

Number: The King is the outward authority of the suit: he names, judges, commands. In TdM the King is enthroned, facing out or three-quarters — the suit's power made public, institutionalised, accountable. His authority is the consolidation of all the suit's energies into governance.
sections.Scene: The Roi de Bâtons (King of Batons) in the Conver 1760 shows a crowned male figure enthroned, holding a knotted club as his scepter of creative authority and looking forward with the confidence of a visionary leader. His crown and court dress in the Marseille primary colors reflect the apex of masculine authority in the creative suit — visionary enterprise and passionate leadership.
sections.Waite:
The physical and emotional nature to which this card is attributed is dark, ardent, lithe, animated,
impassioned, noble. The King uplifts a flowering wand, and wears, like his three correspondences
in the remaining suits, what is called a cap of maintenance beneath his crown. He connects with
the symbol of the lion, which is emblazoned on the back of his throne. Divinatory Meanings : Dark
man, friendly, countryman, generally married, honest and conscientious. The card always signifies
honesty, and may mean news concerning an unexpected heritage to fall in before very long.
Reversed : Good, but severe; austere, yet tolerant.

sections.About the Trumps: Conver's 1760 trumps are the images the whole occult era treated as canonical. This is the deck Court de Gébelin theorised over, Etteilla read from, and Lévi and Wirth re-symbolised. Note the order it fixes: the unnumbered Fool, Justice at VIII, Strength at XI, and Death (XIII) left unnamed — all of which the later Golden Dawn would alter.
sections.How to Use: Read the trumps as the standard reference sequence against which every later esoteric deck defined itself.
sections.Scene: The 22 Major Arcana (Atouts or Trumps) of the Nicolas Conver Tarot de Marseille 1760 form the canonical Marseille trump sequence: the Fool (unnumbered), the Bateleur (I) through the World (XXI). This sequence represents the most definitive surviving reference point for the 'classical' Tarot de Marseille iconographic programme — Nicolas Conver's 1760 woodblock printing is the standard to which all scholarly discussions of the Marseille tradition refer. The Conver Majors reflect centuries of accumulated tradition in the French cardmaking center of Marseille.
sections.Symbol: The 22 Conver Majors — the canonical Marseille allegorical sequence, the most-studied reference point for the classical tarot iconographic programme.

sections.About this Suit: The Coins (Deniers) of the Marseille — the suit of Earth. Its pips are emblematic woodcut arrangements rather than the scenic pictures the later Rider-Waite-Smith would invent.
sections.How to Use: Read the suit for money — using the traditional cartomantic meanings rather than scene-reading.
sections.Scene: The coins suit of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille comprises the Ace through Ten pip cards and four court ranks (Valet, Cavalier, Reine, Roi), totaling 14 cards. In the Conver woodblock printing, the coin pips are rendered as round disc emblems in bold Marseille coloring, and the courts are standing or mounted figures in parti-colored court dress. The coins suit of the Marseille tradition uses the geometric pip arrangement inherited from Italian playing cards.
sections.Symbol: The Conver coins suit — 14 cards in the Marseille pip and court tradition, material resources rendered in the canonical 1760 woodblock style.

The Ace of Cups in the Conver is a particularly elaborate architectural vessel.

sections.Scene: The batons suit of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille comprises the Ace through Ten pip cards and four court ranks, totaling 14 cards. The baton pips are rendered as knotted clubs with characteristic sprouts or knots suggesting living wood — a distinctive Marseille element — and the courts show the four ranks in parti-colored court dress. The knotted organic quality of the Marseille baton distinguishes it from the smooth wands of later occult decks.
sections.Symbol: The Conver batons suit — 14 cards with knotted organic clubs, the creative suit rendered in the canonical 1760 woodblock style.

The Minor Arcana of the Marseille tradition use the pip-card style: suit symbols arranged in geometric patterns rather than the scenic illustrations that later characterize the Rider-Waite-Smith tradition. This geometric pip tradition reflects the Italian and French playing card heritage of the tarot's minor suits.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “marseille” (22).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping identifying all items belonging to the Tarot de Marseille tradition as exemplified by Nicolas Conver's 1760 woodblock deck. 'Marseille' as a label covers the family of related French tarot decks produced in the French playing card center of Marseille from the 17th century onward, of which the Conver 1760 is the canonical reference edition. The Marseille tradition is characterized by its pip-card style, its specific trump iconography, and its four-rank courts (Valet, Cavalier, Reine, Roi).
sections.Symbol: The Marseille tradition — the family of French tarot decks culminating in the canonical 1760 Conver, the reference standard for classical tarot iconography.

The Conver court cards are rendered in the bold woodblock primary colors of the Marseille tradition, the figures in parti-colored court dress against simple backgrounds.
sections.Symbol: Court cards — the four ranks of court figures in each suit, the Marseille hierarchy of Valet, Cavalier, Reine, and Roi.
